blocks.content: A breath passed. Then another.
And then, quietly, without looking at each other—
His hand found hers.
Or hers found his. It didn't matter which.
They fit.

Cassandra's fingers closed around his and he felt something release in his chest that he hadn't known he was holding — some long-tensed thing, some careful arrangement of walls that had been there so long he'd stopped thinking of them as walls. He just felt the release.

Below them, the street moved on. Cars passing. Distant sound. Life doing what life does — continuing carelessly in all directions, indifferent to everything that happened on benches and in galleries and in the private rooms of people trying to figure something out.

And here — on a forgotten bench, under a streetlight that barely reached — two people sat in the particular, quiet, irreversible weight of choosing each other. Not with any word. Not with anything said. Just: this. The hand. The held breath. The willingness to stay.
Ethan looked down at their hands.
He had spent a long time making sure nothing got to matter too much. He had been very good at it. It had cost him things he hadn't let himself count.
He thought about what Nathan had said.
She sounds like the kind of person you meet once.
He thought about the painting. The figure at the window. Choosing to look.
Cassandra's thumb moved slightly against his — the smallest possible motion, not a word but something better than a word. A language that didn't require translation.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Tomorrow.
Her voice was soft. Barely above the sound of the street.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Tomorrow.
Not a question. Not an answer. A continuation.
The city went quiet around them the way cities do when something is happening that has nothing to do with cities.
And for the first time in a long time, Ethan didn't want to be careful.
He just wanted this. Exactly this.
For as long as it was his to hold.
